one_line_takeaway: In a controlled, self-run head-to-head at project scale, five specialized LLM-based vulnerability detectors beat traditional static analyzers on unique-bug coverage but still miss 65-79% of known vulnerabilities, produce false-positive rates averaging 85%+ dominated by shallow interprocedural reasoning and bad source/sink inference, and can burn hundreds of millions of tokens and multi-day runtimes per project.
key_finding: Finding 1: LLM-based methods have low recall on the in-house benchmark (avg 21.09% = 27/128 for C/C++; avg 33.82% = 69/204 for Java) but still surface more unique vulnerabilities than traditional tools (e.g. up to 23 unique cases for a CWE type), driven mainly by mismatched source/sink APIs versus real project code. Finding 2: on real-world projects both LLM-based and traditional tools produce heavy false-alarm rates -- even the best-performing tool averages an 85.3% Sampled False Discovery Rate. Finding 3: across 363 manually coded false positives, Shallow Interprocedural Reasoning (A1) accounted for 37.47% (136/363) and Incorrect Source/Sink Selection (B1) for 19.00% (69/363), with Missed Key Program Points (D1) contributing 46 more; LLM-only failure modes include Incorrect Control Flow reasoning (A2, e.g. RepoAudit conflating mutually-exclusive OS-specific function overloads in vim) and Prompt Deviations (C1). Finding 4: LLM-based detection is computationally expensive -- RepoAudit can consume over 225 million input tokens and 38 million output tokens per project with an average 448-minute (7.5-hour) runtime; LLMDFA needs up to 38,310.33K input / 38,078.26K output tokens and an average 2,000-minute (33-hour) runtime on Java projects (max 4,638.00 minutes), versus seconds-to-minutes for CodeQL/Semgrep.
core_contribution: The paper's own empirical study (not a survey of others' evaluations) is the first project-scale comparison of five specialized LLM-based vulnerability detectors (RepoAudit, KNighter, IRIS, LLMDFA, INFERROI) against two traditional static analyzers (CodeQL, Semgrep). The authors ran all seven tools themselves on (1) an in-house benchmark of 222 known real-world vulnerabilities across 8 CWE types in C/C++ and Java (assembled from ReposVul, CWE-Bench-Java, and JLeaks), and (2) 24 actively maintained open-source projects (e.g. Linux kernel subsystems, ImageMagick, vim, xstream, Jenkins, Keycloak, RxJava, jsoup), manually triaging 385 sampled warnings (>150 human hours) to build an original taxonomy of false-positive root causes, and measuring token/runtime overhead for every tool.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender threat model in the adversarial-ML sense; this is a detection-capability study. The 'attack surface' analyzed is the space of real, already-fixed CWE-401 (memory leak), CWE-416 (use-after-free), CWE-476 (NULL pointer dereference), CWE-022 (path traversal), CWE-078 (OS command injection), CWE-079 (XSS), CWE-094 (code injection), and CWE-772 (missing resource release) vulnerabilities in C/C++ and Java codebases. The paper does not evaluate adversarial evasion of the detectors or prompt-injection against the LLM detectors themselves — it purely measures whether the tools find real known bugs and how often they hallucinate false ones.
limitations: Authors' own stated Threats to Validity: (Internal) FP-taxonomy labeling relies on expert judgment by two authors, mitigated by double coding and reconciliation but still subject to interpretation bias; (External) only 5 LLM-based and 2 traditional tools were studied (chosen for being open-source and project-scale runnable), which may not represent all architectures/prompting paradigms, and the study covers only Java and C/C++, not Rust, Go, TypeScript, or multi-language systems. The in-house dataset recall metric also cannot report precision because the non-vulnerable code space is unlabeled.

datasets_used: In-house benchmark: 222 known vulnerabilities across 8 CWE types (CWE-401, CWE-416, CWE-476 in C/C++; CWE-022, CWE-078, CWE-079, CWE-094, CWE-772 in Java), curated from ReposVul (6,134 CVEs, 236 CWE types, 1,491 projects -- filtered to Linux kernel cases after 2019), CWE-Bench-Java (Maven-buildable Java vulnerabilities), and JLeaks (50 randomly sampled resource-leak cases)

Real-world dataset: 24 actively maintained open-source projects (e.g. linux/sound, linux/mm, linux/net, linux/drivers/net, linux/drivers/peci, ImageMagick, vim, gpac, bitlbee, OpenOLAT, spark, Dspace, xstream, workflow-cps-plugin, tika, xwiki-platform, jenkins, keycloak, onedev, activemq, cron-utils, sql2o, RxJava, jsoup), spanning sizes from ~1.7K to ~6M LOC
quantitative_results: Average recall on C/C++ in-house set: 21.09% (27/128); RepoAudit highest at 55.00% (CWE-401), 10.00% (CWE-416), 28.57% (CWE-476); KNighter/CodeQL/Semgrep: 0.00% on all three C/C++ CWE types

Average recall on Java in-house set: 33.82% (69/204); IRIS: 37.25% (CWE-022), 46.15% (CWE-078), 22.58% (CWE-079), 40.00% (CWE-094); INFERROI: 62.00% on CWE-772 vs CodeQL 10.00%; LLMDFA: 0 detections on Java
62/64, 64/64, 64/64 missed C/C++ vulnerabilities for CodeQL, Semgrep, and KNighter respectively attributed to source/sink API mismatches
Average Sampled False Discovery Rate (SFDR) even for the best-performing tool: 85.3%
False-positive taxonomy over 363 coded FPs: A1 Shallow Interprocedural Reasoning 37.47% (136/363); B1 Incorrect Source/Sink Selection 19.00% (69/363); D1 Missed Key Program Points 46 cases

Token/runtime overhead (Table VII): RepoAudit max 225,493.97K input tokens, max 38,078.26K output tokens, avg runtime 448.31 min (max 2,450.41 min); LLMDFA max 38,310.33K input tokens, max 2,646.89K output tokens, avg runtime 2,000.00 min (max 4,638.00 min); CodeQL avg runtime 21.29 min (max 378.95 min); Semgrep avg runtime 0.64 min (max 2.43 min)
baselines_compared: CodeQL (traditional query-driven static analyzer, 48 curated rules)
Semgrep (traditional pattern-driven static analyzer, 59 curated rules)
